seed_instruction: Write a one-week work schedule for a software development team working on a new app feature. Allocate time for coding, testing, and team meetings.
new_constraint: Do not allocate any time for team meetings in the schedule.
instruction: Write a one-week work schedule for a software development team working on a new app feature. Allocate time for coding, testing, and team meetings. Allocate time for team meetings. Do not allocate any time for team meetings in the schedule.
response:
Here is a one-week work schedule for a software development team focused on a new app feature, with time allocated for coding and testing, and **no time allocated for team meetings**.

This schedule assumes a standard 8-hour workday (9:00 AM - 5:00 PM) and is designed to be highly focused on execution.

---

## 🚀 One-Week Feature Development Schedule

**Goal:** Implement and test the new app feature.
**Team:** Development Team (Assumed roles: Backend, Frontend, QA/Testing)
**Constraint:** No dedicated time slots for team meetings.

### Daily Structure (Monday - Friday)

| Time Slot | Duration | Activity Focus | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **9:00 AM - 10:30 AM** | 1.5 hrs | **Deep Work / Coding Sprint** | Focused implementation on assigned tasks. |
| **10:30 AM - 10:45 AM** | 15 min | *Self-Correction/Quick Sync* | *Informal check-in with immediate pair/buddy if blocked.* |
| **10:45 AM - 12:30 PM** | 1.75 hrs | **Deep Work / Coding Sprint** | Continued feature development. |
| **12:30 PM - 1:30 PM** | 1 hr | **Lunch Break** | Unscheduled break time. |
| **1:30 PM - 3:30 PM** | 2 hrs | **Testing & Integration** | QA testing, unit testing, integration checks, bug fixing. |
| **3:30 PM - 3:45 PM** | 15 min | *Self-Correction/Quick Sync* | *Informal check-in with immediate pair/buddy if blocked.* |
| **3:45 PM - 5:00 PM** | 1.25 hrs | **Refactoring & Documentation** | Code cleanup, writing necessary documentation, preparing for next day. |

---

### Weekly Breakdown by Focus Area

This table shows how the time blocks are generally distributed across the week to ensure a balanced workflow.

| Day | Primary Focus | Coding Allocation (Approx.) | Testing/QA Allocation (Approx.) | Refactoring/Docs Allocation (Approx.) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Monday** | **Setup & Core Logic** | High (Feature scaffolding, initial API endpoints) | Low (Smoke testing of setup) | Medium (Defining acceptance criteria) |
| **Tuesday** | **Feature Implementation** | High (Building out main feature components) | Medium (Unit testing of new modules) | Medium |
| **Wednesday** | **Feature Completion & Integration** | Medium (Finishing remaining logic) | High (Integration testing between components) | Medium |
| **Thursday** | **Intensive Testing & Bug Fixing** | Low (Hotfixes only) | Very High (System testing, regression testing) | Low |
| **Friday** | **Polish, Review, & Handoff** | Low (Minor tweaks) | Medium (Final QA sign-off) | High (Code review prep, documentation finalization) |

### Key Assumptions & Notes

1.  **No Formal Meetings:** This schedule strictly adheres to the constraint of **no allocated time for team meetings** (e.g., Standups, Retrospectives, Planning).
2.  **Informal Communication:** The 15-minute slots are designated for *informal, immediate* communication (e.g., "Hey, I'm stuck on this dependency, can you look at it for 5 minutes?"). This replaces the function of a formal standup.
3.  **Self-Management:** Success in this schedule relies heavily on the team members being highly self-motivated, proactive in identifying blockers, and capable of asynchronous communication (e.g., using Slack/Jira comments instead of waiting for a meeting).
4.  **Task Dependency:** The schedule assumes tasks are broken down granularly enough that developers can work independently for large chunks of time.<turn|>
